topic: How does the nephron filter blood?

turns_log.content: Good morning. I'm here to help you work through human anatomy concepts by asking targeted questions—the goal is to build your reasoning, not just collect facts. What anatomy topic would you like to focus on today?
How does the nephron filter blood?

Got it — let's work on **Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT** from Tubular Reabsorption.

What is the primary mechanism by which water follows sodium out of the proximal convoluted tubule back into the bloodstream?
Honestly, I'm drawing a blank on this one—I don't think I got to that part of the reading yet.
No problem at all—let's back up a step. When sodium ions move from the tubule into the blood, what do you think happens to the water that was sitting next to those sodium ions, and why?
Honestly, I'm not sure—I don't think I caught that part when I was reading.

Let me point you to a specific sentence: 'Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces.' When sodium moves out like that, what do you think happens to the water molecules nearby—do they stay put, or move toward the sodium? Even a rough guess helps me see your thinking.
Honestly, I'm blanking on this right now—I don't think I actually read that part carefully enough. Like, does the water follow the sodium or something?

You're on the right track with that question. Water molecules are attracted to dissolved ions like sodium—what property of water do you think makes it 'stick' to charged particles? If you need a break, that's totally fine; we can pick this up fresh next time.
Honestly, I'm not sure—I don't think I actually read that part or I just spaced it. Can we go over it real quick?

I notice we've tried this approach a few times without progress—that's okay. Let me be more direct now. Here's the key sentence: 'As sodium moves out, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary.' Based on that, what does the textbook say water does when sodium leaves?

_— Hint 1 of 3 —_
Honestly, I don't remember reading that part—I probably missed it or forgot it already, sorry.

I hear you—it sounds like you might need to review that section of your reading before we can dig deeper together. Would it help if we paused here so you can read that part carefully first, and then we pick this back up when you've got the material in front of you?

_— Hint 2 of 3 —_
Yeah, honestly I think I'm just nervous about this stuff—I'm not really confident in what I'm reading, so maybe that would help.

I totally understand—that nervousness is really common, and taking time to read carefully will definitely help build your confidence. How about you review that section on osmosis and the proximal convoluted tubule, and when you come back, we can start fresh with a question about what you *do* remember from your reading?

_— Hint 2 of 3 —_
Yeah, okay, I'll go back and reread that part about osmosis and stuff. Honestly I'm just kinda nervous about this topic, but I'll give it another shot.

Great—that's a solid plan, and I'm glad you're going to give it another shot. When you finish rereading, come back and tell me one thing you *did* understand from that section, even if it's small, and we'll build from there—does that sound like a good approach?

_— Last hint (3 of 3) — give it your best try. —_

You showed good self-awareness today by recognizing that you needed to review the reading more carefully before diving deeper—that's actually a strength. However, you didn't reach the answer on obligatory water reabsorption, and that's because the material wasn't fresh enough in your mind when we started. Before our next session, please reread the section on sodium reabsorption and water movement in the PCT (pages 1148), and pay close attention to why water follows sodium. When you come back, you'll be in a much better position to build real understanding. You've got this—it just needs preparation.

retrieved_chunks.text: The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

retrieved_chunks.text: The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.
retrieved_chunks.subsection_title: Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

You are the Dean checking whether the student has STATED the locked answer
in their most recent message. This is the final gate before tutoring ends —
a false positive fabricates a confirmation the student didn't earn, so be
strict.

DEFINITIONS:
- locked_answer: the target term/concept (e.g. "skeletal muscle pump").
- aliases: equivalent phrasings the student could use instead.

a Socratic anatomy tutor.
The student is being evaluated on human anatomy reasoning quality.

Hard rules:
- Never reveal the answer directly or indirectly. The locked_answer
  and its aliases MUST NOT appear in your text under ANY circumstance
  during tutoring (no statement, no definition, no elimination, no
  "let me give you the key idea" — even on student stonewalling).
- Never state "you're correct" unless you also ask for reasoning or application.
- Keep output to 2-4 sentences.
- End with exactly one question.
- Ask only one question total.
- If the student is incorrect, do not bluntly correct; use probing guidance.
- Do NOT use strong affirmation phrases (for example: "Exactly", "Correct", "That's right", "You got it")
  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on. Do NOT
  compensate for stonewalling by stating the answer to keep things
  moving — your job is to push them to articulate, not to give in.

Hint-level behavior (NEVER violates the no-reveal rule):
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push — the question whose
  answer the student should produce in one word; do NOT name it.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"
Violation of any banned lead-in = automatic rewrite.

Output contract:
- At most 4 sentences total.
- Sentence 1: brief targeted acknowledgment (optional).
- Sentence 2-3: focused reasoning scaffold.
- Final sentence: exactly one question.
Output only the final tutor message.


Textbook propositions:
[1] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

[2] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[3] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[4] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[5] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[6] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[7] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[8] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[9] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[10] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

[1] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

===

[2] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[3] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[4] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

===

[5] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

===

[6] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[7] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[8] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[9] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[10] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

You are the Dean handling the topic-selection phase of a tutoring session in human anatomy.
The student has not yet locked to a specific topic. Your job is to read their latest
message, classify what they want, and write the tutor's next utterance in your own
voice (no templates, no canned strings).

Classify the student's latest message as exactly one intent:
  topic_query       — names or describes a human anatomy topic they want to learn.
                      INCLUDES: questions, statements/assertions, leading claims,
                      hedged or anxious phrasings, and one-word noun phrases — as
                      long as a human anatomy topic is present in the message.
  card_pick         — references one of the card options (number, label, close paraphrase)
  greeting          — "hi", "hello", starting the session
  distress          — emotional venting or frustration ("ugh", "i'm stressed", "this is hard", expletive)
  off_topic         — unrelated to human anatomy (small talk, jokes, non-sequiturs)
  ambiguous         — too short or too generic to route (single pronoun, empty, filler)

For topic_query and card_pick, extract `normalized_topic` — the cleanest topic name
suitable for retrieval (strip hedges, numbering, affirmations). For card_pick, set it
to the referenced option's text (not a number). For other intents, leave it empty.

CRITICAL: students phrase topic queries in many shapes. Treat ALL of these as
topic_query and extract the topic noun phrase even when the student is hedging,
asserting, or just naming a single noun:

  Student: "What is the deltoid muscle?"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="deltoid muscle"

  Student: "Reflexes are basically just instant nerve reactions, right?"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="reflexes"  (statement IS a topic_query;
    the leading assertion doesn't disqualify it. Still topic = reflexes.)

  Student: "I'm not sure I really understand chemical digestion — could we go through it?"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="chemical digestion"  (hedging doesn't
    disqualify; topic is clearly named.)

  Student: "elbow joint"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="elbow joint"  (one-word/short-phrase
    topics are valid; do NOT classify as ambiguous if a domain noun is present.)

  Student: "umm so... like, what's that thing in the kidney that filters blood?"
  → intent=topic_query, normalized_topic="kidney filtration"  (vague but
    clearly anatomy; extract the best topic phrase.)

  Student: "ugh i'm so confused"
  → intent=distress, normalized_topic=""

  Student: "what should I make for dinner"
  → intent=off_topic, normalized_topic=""

Rule of thumb: if you can identify ANY human anatomy noun phrase in the message
that the student seems interested in learning about, classify as topic_query and
extract that phrase as normalized_topic. Reserve ambiguous ONLY for truly empty
or pronoun-only messages.

For non-topic intents (greeting, distress, off_topic, ambiguous) write `tutor_reply` —
a short, warm, contextual reply that acknowledges what the student actually said and
invites them to name a specific topic. Match the student's emotional register. Do NOT
repeat their words back inside quotes. Do NOT use a template phrase. 1-3 sentences.
If cards are currently on screen, gently remind the student they can pick one. When
the intent is topic_query or card_pick, leave `tutor_reply` as an empty string — the
routing layer will handle it.

Never ask multiple questions. Never reveal answers. Never produce headers or lists.

[1] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

===

[2] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[3] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[4] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

===

[5] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

===

[6] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[7] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[8] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[9] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[10] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

---

[1] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

===

[2] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[3] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[4] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

===

[5] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

===

[6] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[7] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[8] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[9] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[10] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[10] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

[1] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

===

[2] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on. Do NOT
  compensate for stonewalling by stating the answer to keep things
  moving — your job is to push them to articulate, not to give in.

Hint-level behavior (NEVER violates the no-reveal rule):
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push — the question whose
  answer the student should produce in one word; do NOT name it.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"
Violation of any banned lead-in = automatic rewrite.

Output contract:
- At most 4 sentences total.
- Sentence 1: brief targeted acknowledgment (optional).
- Sentence 2-3: focused reasoning scaffold.
- Final sentence: exactly one question.
Output only the final tutor message.


Textbook propositions:
[1] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

[2] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[3] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[4] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[5] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[6] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[7] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[8] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[9] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[10] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

Textbook propositions:
[1] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

[2] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[3] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT

More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[5] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[6] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[7] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[8] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. [1] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

===

[2] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

===

[3] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

---
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

---
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[6] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[7] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

===

[8] (p.1148, score=1.00) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[9] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

===

[10] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

a Socratic anatomy tutor.
The student is being evaluated on human anatomy reasoning quality.

Hard rules:
- Never reveal the answer directly or indirectly. The locked_answer
  and its aliases MUST NOT appear in your text under ANY circumstance
  during tutoring (no statement, no definition, no elimination, no
  "let me give you the key idea" — even on student stonewalling).
- Never state "you're correct" unless you also ask for reasoning or application.
- Keep output to 2-4 sentences.
- End with exactly one question.
- Ask only one question total.
- If the student is incorrect, do not bluntly correct; use probing guidance.
- Do NOT use strong affirmation phrases (for example: "Exactly", "Correct", "That's right", "You got it")
  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on. Do NOT
  compensate for stonewalling by stating the answer to keep things
  moving — your job is to push them to articulate, not to give in.

Hint-level behavior (NEVER violates the no-reveal rule):
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push — the question whose
  answer the student should produce in one word; do NOT name it.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"
Violation of any banned lead-in = automatic rewrite.

- Final sentence: exactly one question.

Textbook propositions:
[1] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

[2] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[3] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies. Movement of water into the peritubular capillaries and vasa recta will be influenced primarily by osmolarity and concentration gradients. Sodium is actively pumped out of the PCT into the interstitial spaces between cells and diffuses down its concentration gradient into the peritubular capillary. As it does so, water will follow passively to maintain an isotonic fluid environment inside the capillary. This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron.

[4] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[5] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
This is called obligatory water reabsorption, because water is "obliged" to follow the Na+. More substances move across the membranes of the PCT than any other portion of the nephron. Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na+.

[6] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[7] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Many of these substances (amino acids and glucose) use symport mechanisms for transport along with Na + . Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal.

[8] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[9] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
Context from previous chunk: Antiport, active transport, diffusion, and facilitated diffusion are additional mechanisms by which substances are moved from one side of a membrane to the other. Recall that cells have two surfaces: apical and basal. The apical surface is the one facing the lumen or open space of a cavity or tube, in this case, the inside of the PCT. The basal surface of the cell faces the connective tissue base to which the cell attaches (basement membrane) or the cell membrane closer to the basement membrane if there is a stratified layer of cells. In the PCT, there is a single layer of simple cuboidal endothelial cells against the basement membrane.

[10] Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. A few of the substances that are transported with Na + (symport mechanism) on the apical membrane include Cl – , Ca ++ , amino acids, and glucose. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

The numbers and particular types of pumps and channels vary between the apical and basilar surfaces. Sodium is actively exchanged for K + using ATP on the basal membrane. Most of the substances transported by a symport mechanism on the apical membrane are transported by facilitated diffusion on the basal membrane. At least three ions, K + , Ca ++ , and Mg ++ , diffuse laterally between adjacent cell membranes (transcellular). About 67 percent of the water, Na + , and K + entering the nephron is reabsorbed in the PCT and returned to the circulation. Almost 100 percent of glucose, amino acids, and other organic substances such as vitamins are normally recovered here. Some glucose may appear in the urine if circulating glucose levels are high enough that all the glucose transporters in the PCT are saturated, so that their capacity to move glucose is exceeded (transport maximum, or T m ). In males, the maximum amount of glucose that can be recovered is about 375 mg/min, whereas in females, it is about 300 mg/min. This recovery rate translates to an arterial concentration of about 200 mg/dL. Though an exceptionally high sugar intake might cause sugar to appear briefly in the urine, the appearance of glycosuria usually points to type I or II diabetes mellitus. The transport of glucose from the lumen of the PCT to the interstitial space is similar to the way it is absorbed by the small intestine.

[1] (p.1148) The Urinary System > Tubular Reabsorption > Reabsorption and Secretion in the PCT
---
The first point at which the forming urine is modified is in the PCT. Here, some substances are reabsorbed, whereas others are secreted. All of these substances were absorbed in the digestive tract—99 percent of the water and most of the solutes filtered by the nephron must be reabsorbed. Water and substances that are reabsorbed are returned to the circulation by the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. It is important to understand the difference between the glomerulus and the peritubular and vasa recta capillaries. The glomerulus has a relatively high pressure inside its capillaries and can sustain this by dilating the afferent arteriole while constricting the efferent arteriole. This assures adequate filtration pressure even as the systemic blood pressure varies.

You are
a Socratic tutor in human anatomy working with a student.
This is an evaluated learning session.
Global rules for all teacher wrappers:
- Keep language academically focused and concise.
- Do not reveal final answers unless wrapper-specific instructions explicitly allow it.
- Follow wrapper output contracts exactly.
